Search results for "CS61A Scheme interpreter parser Python PLY":

Title: Project 4 - Scheme Interpreter - CS61A Summer 2019 - GitBook
URL: https://tomthestrom.gitbook.io/cs61a-summer-2019/cs61a-summer-2019/project-4-scheme-interpreter
Content: # Project 4 - Scheme Interpreter. In this project we built an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. I think it's useful for students new to programming to work on a project like this, because it requires them to navigate a "larger" codebase as opposed to the atomic problems that one deals with in the homeworks and labs. Here is a brief overview of each of the Read-Eval-Print Loop components in our interpreter. * **Read**: This step parses user input (a string of Scheme code) into our interpreter's internal Python representation of Scheme expressions (e.g. Pairs). You do not need to read or understand the code for this step. Together, these mutually recursive functions parse Scheme tokens into our interpreter's internal Python representation of Scheme expressions. Code for this step is in the main `scheme.py` file. If the procedure is a user-defined procedure, `scheme_apply` creates a new call frame and calls `eval_all` on the body of the procedure, resulting in a mutually recursive eval-apply loop.

Title: Scheme Interpreter | CS 61A Spring 2026
URL: https://cs61a.org/proj/scheme
Content: # Scheme Interpreter * <scheme.zip>. > * Submit with Phase 1 and Phase 2 complete by **Thursday 04/23** (worth 0.5 pt). In this project, you will use Python to implement an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. The subset of the language used in this project is described in the functional programming section of Composing Programs, as well as this language specification and built-in procedure reference. Examples from Chapters 1 and 2 are included as test cases for this project. Language features from Chapters 3, 4, and 5 are not part of this project, but of course you are welcome to extend your interpreter to implement more of the language. You can download all of the project code as a zip archive. * `scheme_forms.py`: Python functions for evaluating special forms in Scheme (such as `define`, `lambda`, `and`, `cond`, etc.). * `questions.scm`: Scheme procedures for you to implement.

Title: cs61a-projects/scheme/scheme.py at master - GitHub
URL: https://github.com/MattZhao/cs61a-projects/blob/master/scheme/scheme.py
Content: """A Scheme interpreter and its read-eval-print loop.""". def scheme\_eval(expr, env, \_=None): # Optional third argument is ignored. >>> expr = read\_line("(+ 2 2)"). >>> scheme\_eval(expr, create\_global\_frame()). return scheme\_atomp(expr) or scheme\_stringp(expr) or expr is okay. def scheme\_apply(procedure, args, env):. """Apply Scheme PROCEDURE to argument values ARGS in environment ENV.""". return apply\_primitive(procedure, args, env). new\_env = make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env). def eval\_all(expressions, env):. """Evaluate a Scheme list of EXPRESSIONS & return the value of the last.""". scheme\_eval(expressions[x], env). return scheme\_eval(expressions[len(expressions)-1], env, True). def apply\_primitive(procedure, args\_scheme\_list, env):. """Apply PrimitiveProcedure PROCEDURE to ARGS\_SCHEME\_LIST in ENV. args = list(args\_scheme\_list) # Convert a Scheme list to a Python list. def make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env):. """Make a frame that binds the formal paramters of PROCEDURE to ARGS.""". new\_f = procedure.env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). new\_f = env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). in a Scheme list of formal parameters FORMALS are bound to the Scheme. a Scheme list of BODY expressions, and a parent environment that. env.bindings[target] = scheme\_eval(expressions[1], env). if scheme\_true(scheme\_eval(expressions[0], env)):. return scheme\_eval(expressions[1],env, True).

Title: r/berkeley on Reddit: Is CS61A still using Scheme as its main teach ...
URL: https://www.reddit.com/r/berkeley/comments/hl9rxt/is_cs61a_still_using_scheme_as_its_main_teach
Content: # Is CS61A still using Scheme as its main teach resource or has them switched to Python? Skip to main contentIs CS61A still using Scheme as its main teach resource or has them switched to Python? Hi, I have heard of the CS61A and its great reputation and have been thinking about taking this course. However, I was told that the instructing language has been changed from Scheme to Python. I am a little worried because I also read SICP( **Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs**) at the same time in which Scheme is the language used to instruct, fearing that there might be some gaps of knowledge between the contents of the book and the lectures. Python is the primary language, but scheme is taught in the last third of the course. You will learn Scheme inside and out since the last project of CS61A is implementing a Scheme interpreter in Python.

Title: Solution of Proj4. Scheme Interpreter of CS61A (2021-Fall)
URL: https://martinlwx.github.io/en/proj4-scheme-interpreter-of-cs61a-of-ucb-fa21
Content: > Implement the `define` and `lookup` methods of the Frame class…`bindings` is a dictionary representing the bindings in the frame…`parent` is the parent Frame instance…The *environment* for a `Frame` instance consists of that frame, its parent frame, and all its ancestor frames, including the Global Frame. To implement the `define` function, you just need to save the `{ symbol: value }` in `self.bindings`. ...def define(self, symbol, value): def define(self, symbol, value): def define(self, symbol, value): """Define Scheme SYMBOL to have VALUE."""  """Define Scheme SYMBOL to have VALUE.""" """Define Scheme SYMBOL to have VALUE.""" self.bindings[symbol] = value  self.bindings[symbol] = value self. Errors if SYMBOL is not found."""  """Return the value bound to SYMBOL. we check if the symbol is in the current frame  # Case 1. we check if the symbol is in the current frame if symbol in self.bindings.keys():  if symbol in self.bindings.keys(): if symbol in self.
